Walk-Forward Validation: SDC 2024 vs. 2026 Method

SDC 2024 vs. 2026 Cohort-Component Method | Origins: 2005, 2010, 2015, 2020 | Data: Annual Resolution | Generated March 03, 2026
Legend:
SDC 2024
2026 Method
Actual (Census/PEP)

Key Takeaways

  • SDC 2024 wins at the state level in 18 of 19 forecast horizons, though margins are often narrow.
  • Short-horizon accuracy (1-5 years): SDC 3.0% MAPE vs 2026 3.1% MAPE. Long-horizon (10+ years): SDC 20.2% vs 2026 23.0%.
  • Both methods systematically under-project North Dakota's population at all horizons, driven by the unpredicted Bakken oil boom (2007-2014).
  • County-level average |% error|: SDC 9.1% vs 2026 8.9%. The 2026 method has a modest advantage at the county level.
  • Most recent origin (2020): SDC MAPE 0.4% vs 2026 MAPE 0.4% over a 4-year forecast window, reflecting performance under stable conditions.

Methodology Summary

SDC 2024 Method

  • 5-year cohort-component projection steps
  • Uses census-to-census residual migration rates
  • Static fertility and mortality assumptions
  • Simpler approach with fewer parameters

2026 Enhanced Method

  • Annual projection steps with convergence schedule
  • PEP-recalibrated residual migration with GQ correction (ADR-055)
  • Lee-Carter mortality improvement model
  • BEBR-style multi-period migration averaging (ADR-036)
  • Ward County floor constraint (ADR-052)
  • Reservation county PEP anchoring (ADR-045)

Walk-Forward Validation Design

  • Origins: 2005, 2010, 2015, 2020 — each projects forward through 2024
  • Evaluation: compare projected population to actual Census/PEP data
  • Metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) and Mean Percentage Error (MPE)
  • Levels: state-level (53 counties summed) and individual county-level

Projection Curves vs. Actual Population (State Level)

Each panel shows projections from a different origin year. Solid lines show the validated portion (through 2024); dashed lines show future projections. The black dashed line is actual Census/PEP population.

State-Level Error Trajectory by Origin Year

Signed percentage error at the state level over increasing forecast horizons. The shaded band shows the +/-5% acceptable zone. Negative values indicate under-projection.

Average Error by Forecast Horizon

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) at the state level, averaged across all applicable origin years for each horizon. Lower is better.

Method Delta: Where Does 2026 Improve Over SDC?

Difference in Mean Absolute Percentage Error: (2026 Method MAPE) minus (SDC 2024 MAPE). Negative values (below zero) mean the 2026 method has lower error. Positive values mean SDC 2024 has lower error.

Method Comparison: County Error Scatter

Each point is one county at one origin-year and horizon. Points below the diagonal mean the 2026 method had lower absolute error than SDC; points above mean SDC was better. Use the origin-year buttons to filter.

Summary

  • 2026 Method wins in 1564 of 2438 county-horizon combinations (64%)
  • SDC 2024 wins in 874 combinations (36%)
  • Ties: 0
  • Diamond markers indicate Bakken oil-patch counties

County Error Distributions

Histogram of county-level absolute percentage errors across all origin-year and horizon combinations. A tighter, left-shifted distribution indicates better performance. Use the dropdown to filter by horizon range.

County-Level Error Heatmap

Mean Absolute Percentage Error for each county across origin/horizon combinations. Only the 20 counties with the highest average error are shown. Counties are sorted by their overall MAPE (worst at top).

County Error Difference Heatmap

Difference in Mean Absolute Percentage Error: m2026 MAPE − SDC MAPE. Blue = 2026 method has lower error (better), Red = SDC 2024 has lower error (better), White = no meaningful difference. Counties are sorted by overall average difference (most improved by m2026 at top).

County Deep-Dive Explorer

Select a county from the dropdown to see its projection trajectories and error patterns across all origin years. The left panel shows projected vs actual population; the right panel shows signed percentage error by horizon.

Summary of which method performs better across different forecast horizon ranges, plus identification of horizons where both methods struggle (>10% state error).

Direction of Error Analysis

Mean Percentage Error (signed, averaged across origins) showing whether methods systematically over- or under-project. Negative values mean under-projection. This chart reveals systematic bias in both methods.

Interpretation

  • Both methods under-project (negative MPE) at all horizons, reflecting the unexpected Bakken oil boom that drove rapid population growth from 2005-2015.
  • The bias grows with horizon length, reaching -25% or more at 19-year horizons from the 2005 origin.
  • For more recent origins (2015, 2020), the bias is dramatically smaller, suggesting both methods perform well when not confronted with unprecedented structural economic shifts.
